observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Municipal Treasurer to update and reconcile the Cashbook balances with the Ledger balances of the CIB accounts and promptly communicate to the Municipal Accountant any discrepancies identified for inclusion in the BRS or for proper adjustments in the accounting records;
Municipal Accountant to regularly prepare and submit a three-way BRS for all active bank accounts maintained by the Municipality to ensure the reconciliation of balances among the ledger, cashbook and bank records, and prevent the accumulation of variances; and
Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to regularly coordinate and reconcile their records related to the CIB accounts to ensure the accuracy of balances and support the fair presentation of the FS.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Strictly monitor and require the AOs/employees to liquidate their due and demandable cash advances with complete supporting documents within the prescribed period; and
Strictly impose the sanctions for non-compliance as duly provided under Section 122 of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445 which states “Unjustified failure on the part of the official or employee concerned to submit the documents and reports mentioned herein shall be considered a ground for the automatic suspension of payment of this salary until he shall have complied with the aforesaid requirements, without prejudice to any disciplinary action that may be instituted against him.”

We recommended the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant:
To strictly monitor the liquidation of cash advances and require the accountable officers/employees to liquidate their cash advances within the prescribed period before availing additional cash advance; and
To discontinue granting additional cash advances to employees with unliquidated cash advances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Municipal Accountant to update its record of acquisition/disposal/transfer of PPEs in the PPELCs and ensure that the total balance of PPELCs tally with the balances of controlling PPE accounts in the General Ledger. Subsequently, submit the same to the GSO;
GSO to compare the RPCPPE submitted to the Inventory Committee dated June 30, 2024 with the updated PPELCs from the Municipal Accountant and thereafter, arrive with the list of PPE items which are recorded in the PPELCs but not included in the RPCPPE; and
Inventory Committee to come up with an updated list of PPE items found at station and list of non-existing/missing PPEs using the additional working paper containing the list of PPE items which are recorded in the PPELCs but not included in the RPCPPE. Also, to update the ICF by including the updated list of PPEs found at station and submit to the Audit Team the revised RPCPPE with the current condition per PPE item indicated in the “remarks” column.
We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GSO to:
Renew all PARs to reflect the new property numbers assigned by the Inventory Committee;
Prepare the IIRUP by including the PPE items which were found to be unserviceable and/or no longer in use, as of date of inspection; and,
Inform the Head of the Agency about the non-existing/missing PPEs without pending Request for Relief and prepare letters addressed to each concerned accountable officer/personnel demanding the production of the PPE he/she is accountable for.
As to the non-existing/missing PPEs for which accountability could not be established despite exhaustion of all diligent efforts, we recommended that the Management follow procedures provided under Section 7.11 of COA Circular No. 2020-006.
Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant provide additional disclosures on PPE in the Notes to financial statements by presenting information on the total amount of non-existing/missing PPEs included in the PPE balance and PPEs found at station which are still needing appraisal, supported with breakdown as to sub-major PPE account group, in consonance with Section 9.2 of the same Circular.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Properly recognize the semi-expendable property by using the list of new accounts as provided under Annex B of COA Circular No. 2024-006 dated March 14, 2024; and
Record in the books the following adjusting journal entries:
Special Education Fund Books
Identify the procurement and issuance of other semi-expendable machinery and equipment during CY 2024 and effect adjustments in the books.
We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GSO and the Municipal Accountant to strictly adhere with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2022-004 dated May 31, 2022, by ensuring the preparation and maintenance of the prescribed forms, registry, and reports to strengthen the controls and safeguards over semi-expendable properties.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Municipal Accountant to:
•	prepare the following journal entry to properly recognize the DRRM funds from other LGUs:
Trust Fund Books
•	disclose in the Notes to the Financial Statements the financial assistance received from Other LGUs for DRRM activities.
Municipal DRRMO to ensure that all supporting documents relative to any assistance/grant for DRRM be provided to the Municipal Accountant for proper recognition in the books; and
Local Sanggunian to immediately appropriate the financial assistance for DRRM-related activities.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to reflect in the original budget column of the current year’s SCBAA the current and continuing appropriation ending balances of previous year’s CO in accordance with IPSAS 24 and Section 322 of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
HRMO and Municipal Accountant to:
Exert additional effort to reconcile beginning balances of the inter-agency payable accounts – Due to GSIS, Due to Pag-IBIG, and Due to PhilHealth related to prior years’ transactions and address identified discrepancies for the current year, implement the necessary adjustments and promptly remit any outstanding balances; and
Strictly abide with the provisions of RA No. 8291, RA No. 9769 and Revised IRR of RA No. 7875 in order to avoid administrative sanctions.
HRMO to:
Ensure timely submission of updated information of the Municipal employees to the concerned agencies, such as new salary rates, employment status, position, and such other pertinent information that will take effect on the particular due month and accordingly pay the amount based on the updated salary rate; and
Designate a separate HRM staff member to handle coordination and reconciliation with the Accounting Office to ensure that the monthly premiums and loan deductions from the payrolls/disbursement vouchers accurately match the amount to be remitted to GSIS, Pag-IBIG, and PhilHealth;

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD focal person to:
Facilitate the update and maintenance of the GAD Database using sex-disaggregated data from the Community-Based Monitoring System and other relevant sources, in compliance with Annex B and Section C.2 of the JMC to ensure that identified gender issues are evidence-based and reflective of actual conditions;
Institutionalize a more rigorous evaluation process for proposed GAD PPAs by requiring the use of gender analysis tools such as the HGDG and ensuring alignment with documented gender issues as required under Sections C.1.4 and C.4 of the JMC. Proposals that do not address gender gaps should be revised or excluded from the GAD Plan; and
Strengthen the functionality of the GAD Focal Point System by providing adequate support and capacity-building, and by establishing internal mechanisms to harmonize interdepartmental proposals with GAD mandates to ensure that only compliant and gender-responsive programs are included in the final Plan.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to:
Cause the conduct of investigation to establish property accountability over these semi-expendable items and thereafter, prepare and issue ICS to the accountable persons;
Direct the GSO to ensure that the existing PARs for PPEs reclassified as semi-expendable properties be replaced with ICS; and
Direct the Municipal Administrator to formulate and disseminate an administrative policy on the proper turnover of documents, requiring all retiring, resigning or reassigned/reshuffled employees to prepare a complete inventory of their accountabilities, including any unfinished duties and responsibilities, which must be duly acknowledged and receipted by the incoming officers.

We reiterated our prior recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Municipal Engineer, in coordination with the MPDC, and with the assistance of the department heads involved in the proposed infrastructure projects, thoroughly plan and identify feasible projects that comply with the provisions of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 2020-001 by preparing the initial POW, to ensure the optimal utilization of funds and to prevent the inclusion of projects that may be discontinued due to inadequate procurement planning; and
MPDC to endorse the PPAs with accurate project titles and specific locations to the MDC for incorporation into the Supplemental AIP or appropriate Ordinance, which will authorize the Municipal Mayor to enter into contracts on behalf of the LGU.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor ensure that the MDRRMC has convened and agreed on matters concerning LDRRMF by securing the signatures of the members in every MDRRMC resolution.
We likewise recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MDRRMC to annually review and update the LDRRMFIP and include the activities to be funded from the unexpended QRF and DRRMF-MOOE of the previous years which were transferred to the STF.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
MENRO to:
Include in their annual PPMP, the procurement of personal protective equipment sufficient for a year, to protect the drivers and collectors directly involved in the solid waste management; and
Enforce stringent policies on proper wearing of personal protective equipment. Clear directives must be given to ensure that the complete set of personal protective equipment is mandatorily worn all throughout the collection process to safeguard their health and well-being;
MHO, in coordination with the MENRO, to provide training in compliance with Section 23 (b) of RA No. 9003 for the garbage collectors and other personnel directly dealing with the collection of solid wastes, through the TESDA Garbage Collector NC 1 training program to equip them with the necessary skills on the proper handling of wastes and avoid exposure to possible health risks and injuries; and
MENRO and MHO to include barangays operating their own garbage collection trucks in meetings or trainings that would educate them on the proper handling of solid waste to ensure that all levels of local waste management operations adhere to health, safety, and environmental standards, and promote uniform compliance with the provisions of RA No. 9003.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC to:
Conduct a thorough and in-depth post qualification procedure prior to awarding any contract, to ensure that all statements, representations, and documents submitted by the bidder with the LCB are properly verified, validated and confirmed to be true and correct; and
Ensure that the Post Qualification Evaluation Report clearly outlines the methodologies conducted to determine that the bidder possesses the legal, technical and financial capability to undertake the multiple projects awarded to them.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the:
End-user and GSO to:
Ensure that all deliveries strictly conform with the specifications stated in the PO, unless an approved amendment by the HoPE is issued; and
Implement a post-delivery inspection and acceptance process to confirm compliance with the terms and conditions of the contract;
BAC, through the BAC-TWG and Secretariat, implement stricter post-qualification process of bidders to ensure their capacity, legitimacy, and willingness to deliver goods and services as specified;
End-users and concerned staff to stop the practice directly procuring the meals and snacks from other establishments other than the supplier; and
End-users, with the assistance of the BAC, to exercise due prudence in the procurement process by adhering to the principles of accountability and economy, and ensuring that goods are procured at reasonable and equitable prices from bonafide suppliers.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Concerned officials to immediately discontinue the practice of charging expenditures for meals and snacks during staff meetings and regular sessions to government funds. If the provision of meals and snacks is deemed necessary, the expense should be charged against the officials’ Representation Allowance; and
Staff of the SBO to strictly monitor and ensure that attendance sheets are filled-out and signed only during the actual conduct of the event, in order to accurately reflect the true number of attendees and to uphold the integrity of attendance records.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Municipal Engineer and MPDC to carefully plan and identify the programs and projects to be implemented, ensuring that these are eligible and authorized in accordance with the provisions of RA No. 8240, as amended by RA No. 10351, and as further amended by RA No. 11346; and
Municipal Accountant to strictly monitor the proper utilization and disbursement of the RA No. 8240 shares of the LGU, in accordance with applicable budgeting, accounting, and auditing rules and regulations.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the MPDC to:
Consider, when planning, existing conditions and other factors in the determination of realistic contract time to ensure that projects are implemented within a reasonable target completion date;
Give time and prioritize the review, revisions and approval of the deliverables to avoid further delays; and
Ensure that terms of the contract are strictly followed, and provisions for contract performance monitoring, contract remedies and specific roles and responsibilities of each party will be clearly stated to avoid delays in the implementation of the project.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer and the MPDC to:
Create a Monitoring and Evaluation Team (M&E) to monitor and evaluate the operational status of the program; and
Prioritize the funding of the rehabilitation of these constructed water systems based on the comprehensive assessment of the M&E Team.
We also recommended the inclusion in the agenda during the conduct of Municipal Development Council meetings and barangay consultations, the issues and concerns, and to discuss responsive and sustainable solutions regarding the water system.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the:
Municipal GSO to conduct:
A feasibility study to identify a possible area or location for the establishment of a garage or motor pool, for the protection of the Municipality’s vehicles from unnecessary exposure to environmental elements and minimizing the risk of theft. The resulting study should then be submitted to the Municipal Development Council for review and appropriate action; and
An in-depth inquiry into the circumstances surrounding the eight vehicles that became unserviceable prior to reaching their estimated useful lives, with objective of determining the underlying causes of premature deterioration and identify any instances of negligence, misuse, or procurement-related issues.
All Department Heads to:
Assign an official driver for each municipal vehicle, who shall be responsible for its operation, maintenance and cleanliness, in order to establish accountability and ensure proper care; and
Be reminded that they remain responsible for overseeing the condition of vehicles assigned to their respective offices, even if they are not formally listed as the AOs.
BAC and end-users for future vehicle acquisitions, to consider the availability of spare parts and the long-term maintenance costs to avoid encountering similar issues experienced with the Mahindra and Foton vehicles.
GSO to:
Ensure that all government vehicles are clearly marked with "FOR OFFICIAL USE ONLY" along with the Municipality’s logo, to reinforce their intended purpose and prevent unauthorized use; and
See to it that all newly acquired vehicles are properly marked prior to their release to the respective offices.
Further, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor consider the hiring of qualified mechanic to manage the motor pool, ensuring proper maintenance, repair, and overall care of the municipal vehicles.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that the monthly salary rate utilized in the computation of honoraria for lecturers, resource persons, coordinators, and facilitators is adequately substantiated with appropriate proof of compensation such as, but not limited to, payslips, certificates of latest compensation from the Human Resource Department, or other official records.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to withhold expanded withholding tax of either 5% or 10% on honoraria paid to Lecturers, Resource Persons, Coordinators and Facilitators pursuant to RR No. 11-2018 dated March 15, 2018.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the Municipal Accountant to stop the practice of paying honoraria to agency personnel who are lecturers/resource persons of training and similar programs and activities by virtue of their positions in the sponsoring agency.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Human Resource Management Officer to prioritize the allocation of funds  in the budget proposal for the creation, filling and hiring of additional personnel of mandatory positions under the LDRMMO, as prescribed in JMC No. 2014-1 to enhance the Municipality’s capacity to effectively fulfill its mandate of promoting disaster risk resilience and climate change adaptation, ultimately contributing to a safer, more resilient, and vigilant community.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, in coordination with the Municipal Engineer, direct the:
Project Engineers to:
Strictly require all contractors to ensure that their entire workforce is wearing the required safety gears or personal protective equipment at all times within the construction project site for the protection and welfare of both the construction workers and the general public;
Closely monitor the proper implementation of the CSHP and Standards by the contractors at the project sites by maintaining detailed records of contractor’s non-compliance per site visitation, which can be referenced during future payment processes; and
Municipal Administrator to formulate an internal policy that clearly defines the applicable sanctions or penalties for contractors found to be in violation of the CSHP, in order to ensure accountability and reinforce adherence to established safety protocols.
Municipal Accountant to require the submission of complete documents as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2012-001 before effecting payment.
Further, we recommend that the Municipal Engineer standardize the computation of the costing of CSHP to ensure consistency, transparency and accountability and to prevent manipulation of project costs.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the following:
The Municipal Assessor to reconcile records with the Municipal Treasurer by providing a copy of the complete and updated list of assessed value of taxable and exempt RPUs to the latter;
The Municipal Treasurer to use the list provided by the Municipal Assessor in updating the RPTAR/TIC as basis in the preparation of the revised list of RPT receivables and furnish the revised list to the Municipal Accountant at the beginning of the year; and,
The Municipal Accountant to prepare the following correcting entries:
GF Books
Debit		Deferred Real Property Tax
Credit		Real Property Tax Receivable
Amount	₱7,969,656.52
Particulars: To derecognize the RPT Receivable for CY 2024, net of 	advance payment, and the accrued penalties from the Certified 	Statement of Delinquency Summary amounting to ₱2,890,047.30 and 	₱5,079,609.22, respectively.
SEF Books
Debit		Deferred Special Education Tax
Credit		Special Education Tax Receivable
Amount	₱7,969,656.52
Particulars: To derecognize the SET Receivable for CY 2024, net of 	advance payment, and the accrued penalties from the Certified 	Statement of Delinquency Summary amounting to ₱2,890,047.30 and 	₱5,079,609.22, respectively.
d. The Municipal Accountant to reconcile the revised list of RPT receivables from the initially submitted QRPPA from the Municipal Assessor before recognizing the CY 2024 RPT and SET Receivable at the beginning of the following year.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor create an Inventory Committee to complete the physical inventory of all PPEs of the Municipality in three months or less as initial step in the availment of one-time cleansing of PPE.
Further, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the following:
The Municipal Accountant to prepare the following adjusting entries:
Municipal Engineer to furnish copies of GPPA Report as of year-end to the Municipal Accountant as basis in fairly presenting the actual accomplishments for infrastructure projects.
Property and Accounting Units and the Inventory Committee to undertake all procedures prescribed under COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 to come up with the reconciled and correct balances of PPE accounts in the financial statements.
GSO to expedite the disposal of unserviceable properties to free up storage spaces, prevent further deterioration of PPE and generate additional income from sale.
Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to comply with the disclosure requirements on the significant changes and breakdown of material balances of PPE to ensure that relevant information is available to the intended users of the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that the MDRRMC has convened and agreed on matters concerning LDRRMF by securing the signatures of the members in every MDRRMC resolution.
Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the communication letter for the needed reciprocal entry in the BFP’s books for proper asset recognition and to establish accountability and prepare the following correcting entry in the GF books:

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor/HOPE direct the:
BAC to revisit DBM Budget Circular No. 2007-3 dated November 29, 2007, particularly the updated funding source for payment of honoraria and overtime pay to government personnel involved in government procurement, and exclude LD in the total computation of available funds for BAC honoraria; and,
Municipal Accountant in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer to constantly coordinate and improve monitoring controls, particularly in the payment of BAC honoraria, such as maintenance of subsidiary ledgers, in order to ensure that funds are sufficient to cover the said expenses.
We recommended further that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC and Municipal Accountant to refrain from paying BAC Honoraria out of the excess collections from the proceeds of the sale of bid documents of the previous year and to adhere strictly to the provisions of DBM Budget Circular No.  2004-5A dated October 7, 2005 and GPPB Resolution No. 18-2012 to ensure that the payment is on a legal basis.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to:
Immediately transfer the cash items, totaling ₱28,572,030.50, to its appropriate funds;
Continue to trace the unreconciled balances; and,
Conduct regular reconciliation of the accounts to ensure that the balances of reciprocal accounts are the same at all times.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Municipal Accountant to record all purchases of supplies and materials regardless of whether they are consumed or distributed during the year as inventory following the Perpetual Inventory Method; and
GSO to prepare the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) from the consolidated weekly Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) which will be the basis of the Municipal Accountant in recording the expenses using the appropriate expenditure accounts.
We also recommended that the Inventory Committee conduct physical count of inventories, prepare the RPCI and the maintenance of Supplies Ledger Cards and the Stock Cards by the Municipal Accountant and the GSO to ascertain the existence and condition of inventories.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the End-User Department Heads to fast track the completion of the required supporting documents by communicating with the concerned departments and/or suppliers, taking actions to address the cause of the delay in the process, and strictly monitor all unpaid obligations to ensure that deficiencies are addressed promptly and paid in a timely manner in accordance with the terms agreed upon in the Purchase Order or contracts and the standard operating procedures provided in the system procedures and/or Citizen’s Charter.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Local School Board (LSB) to ensure that the SEF Budget of the Municipality is presented by P/A/P, by allotment class and per school using SEF Budget Preparation Form No. 1 (Annex A) in the ensuing years and that neither lump-sum appropriations nor appropriations with generic terms be included in the said budget to better address the needs of all public schools in the Municipality; and,
Municipal Budget Officer to assist the LSB in incorporating in their Budget the additional allowable expenses enumerated under Item 1.0 of DepEd-DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2020 to support the holistic utilization of the Special Education Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Municipal Engineer, in coordination with the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and with the assistance of the different department heads involved with the proposed infrastructure projects, will thoroughly plan and identify projects that are feasible and compliant with DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 2020-001, thus ensuring optimal utilization of funds and preventing the inclusion of projects prone to discontinuance caused by inadequate procurement planning; and,
Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to endorse the PPAs with the correct project titles and location to the Municipal Development Council (MDC) for incorporation into the Supplemental AIP, Supplemental Budget, and Appropriation Ordinance that will give prior authorization for the Municipal Mayor to enter into contracts on behalf of the LGU.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) and the Municipal Agriculturist to prioritize and ensure that the PPAs programmed from the shares of collections of tobacco excise taxes are exclusively intended for the benefit of NTA-registered tobacco farmers.
We also recommended that the MPDC and the Municipal Agriculturist in coordination with the barangay council to conduct public consultations or assemblies with the tobacco farmers to facilitate and provide tailored support and assistance that will address their needs effectively.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
end-users to prepare the technical specifications of their requested equipment with the assistance of the BAC as part of their procurement planning and determination of ABC;
BAC-TWG, being the technical experts, to improve and standardize preparation of specifications as required in Section 17 of the revised IRR of RA 9184; and
BAC Secretariat to consolidate the PPMPs of the end-users in order to reduce the unnecessary, excessive and duplicitous items, thus varying prices of same items can be avoided.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC to submit a written justification on the delays in the posting of NOA and NTP in the PhilGEPS website and to ensure compliance with Sections 37.1.6 and 37.4.2 in the posting of NOA and NTP in the PhilGEPS.
We also recommend immediate submission of the Certification from the Head of the NOA, Contract and NTP were posted at any conspicuous place on the premises of the procuring entity. Further, we recommend that the BAC comply with their jury duty in adherence to the principles of transparency, accountability and good governance in the government procurement process.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor comply with the provisions of Section 16 of the IRR of RA No. 9433, such as: 1.) preparing a list of positions in its plantilla with the corresponding job description or actual duties that describes the basis for justifying such positions for those who are exposed to occupational risks and assigned to places as stated in Section 16; and 2.) establishing a procedure for identifying personnel who shall be exposed to hazardous and occupational risks as stated in the Section, including the duration of exposure.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant that claims for hazard pay of PSW and PSWDW be supported with proof of actual service rendered during the period being claimed and that the same be computed in accordance with the provisions in Section 16 of the IRR of RA No. 9433.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
Payroll master to prepare a separate payroll for specific-purpose allowances such as RATA, Hazard Pay, and Subsistence and Laundry Allowance, and exclude the same from the general payroll in order to accurately evaluate the financial stability of employees in availing of personal loans;
HRD to strictly monitor payroll deductions by maintaining 	employee ledgers as the basis for approval of loan applications and refrain from signing certifications for loan 	applications should an employee’s net take-home pay fall below the mandatory amount of P5,000.00, exclusive of 	specific-purpose allowances; and
HRD to conduct an extensive information campaign for all employees to ensure a comprehensive understanding of loan-related matters, empower employees with essential knowledge, and promote financial literacy across the organization.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Person to:
Strengthen the data gathering and collating mechanism of the LGU GAD database so gender issues in the Municipality  can be more readily determined and addressed; and
Familiarize herself with HGDG and use the tool to strategically plan on the PPAs to be included in the GAD Plan, to ensure that the PPAs have been evaluated as to their responsiveness to the gender issues of the LGU and accordingly, as to the corresponding budget for the year of the PPAs that may be attributed to the GAD budget.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to allocate 1% of the IRA for LCPC pursuant to Section 15 of RA 9344 and 1% of the LGU budget for Persons with Disability and Senior Citizens pursuant to DILG Memorandum Circular 2014-59 to accommodate the PPAs budgeted under the GAD which pertains to these special purpose appropriations.

We recommended that Management properly plan their future procurement by being updated and well-versed with relevant issuances affecting their projects.  In cases where the program is spearheaded by another government agency, Management shall coordinate closely with said partner agency to ensure compliance with rules and regulation and take advantage of the assistance being offered to maintain economy and efficiency in the use of public resources.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor initiate the revision of their Tax Ordinance to incorporate advance collection of business taxes.
We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Municipal Treasurer to stop the practice of collecting in advance the business taxes from contractors. Instead, collect business taxes on or before January 20 of the subsequent year; and,
Municipal Accountant to adjust the affected accounts in the 	financial statements with the following correcting entry:
Account Title	Business Tax	₱1,552,445.49
Other Deferred Credits	₱1,552,445.49

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Inspectorate Team to expedite the inspection of the project and prepare the punch-list to be furnished to the contractor to address the identified issues and deficiencies ensuring the system to be fully functional; and,
Municipal Accountant to recalculate the LDs; thereafter,  	demand and collect the same from the contractor.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Management direct the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board to provide for schemes that would improve solid waste management in the municipality and strictly comply with the following:

Strictly enforce waste segregation at source in order to achieve the primary objective to protect the environment and public health from potential hazard of solid wastes;

Promote Solid Waste Management Awareness through proactive Information and Education Campaigns (IEC) in coordination with the Barangay Solid Waste Management Committees and academic institutions; and

Monitor the constructed MRFs in different areas and barangays within the Municipality to see if these facilities are operating effectively and serve its intended purpose.
observations.managementAction:
Designated place and time to collect “basura”

Provision of color-coded trash cans especially for hazardous solid waste in government offices as well as in private establishments in accordance with Safety Seal Requirements. Conducted inspections in barangay premises, private establishments and other offices within the mandate of the LGU – Safety Seal.

Containment of hospital and covid-related wastes in authorized and secured bins while finalizing special arrangement with LUMC regarding their collection.
Prepared and currently implementing MRF Monitoring Plan through a focal/assigned personnel
observations.reason:
The practice of waste segregation was not being observed in the municipality since garbage truck collections are not scheduled as to waste category to be picked-up on a given day. No garbage collection system was being implemented; only schedule per barangay was being scheduled. The lack of awareness on segregation resulted in dumping everything altogether.
No policies are crafted to enforce waste segregation at source, which is mandatory under Republic Act No. 9003 or the Ecological Waste Management Act, with local governments supposedly in charge. 
Information dissemination regarding solid waste management in households was not prioritized as evidenced by rampant indiscreet burning of household waste. The MRFs in the barangay was not being utilized as to its intended use but was merely constructed for compliance. The barangay constituents were not aware of the proper use of the MRF. They perceived it as a “trash can”.

15.22. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Bids and Awards Committee to:
Ensure that the APP is completely filled up following the step-by-step guide found in the GPPB website under downloadable forms which is in accordance with the pertinent provisions of RA No. 9184 and GPPB Circular No. 07-2015.

Adopt Competitive Bidding (Public Bidding) as the general mode of procurement and only resort to Alternative Modes if all the conditions set forth under Annex H of the RIRR of RA 9184 are met; and
Capacitate and keep themselves abreast concerning updates on procurement law and preparation of APP and apply it in the discharge of their functions.

We recommended that the Local Chief of Executive being the Head of the Procuring Entity require the Bids and Awards Committee Secretariat to prepare and upload their yearly APP-CSE in the PhilGEPS within the prescribed period to support the government policy on the efficient and economical procurement process.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management:
Obtain the prior authorization of the Sanggunian before acting on behalf of the Municipality especially on fiscal matters involving the acceptance and utilization of donated funds.
Direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Recognize the unrecorded cash donations amounting to P700,000.00 and its subsequent utilization in the Trust Fund Book and make the necessary correcting entries made for the improperly recorded cash donations amounting to P216,000.00;
Ensure that the details of cash donations are disclosed in the Notes to the Financial Statements as required under Item VII (2)(c) of COA Circular No. 2014-002;
Direct the Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to adhere with the accounting and reporting guidelines of donations required under Section 5.1.12 of Item V and Annex C of COA Circular No. 2012-002 and Item VII(2)(c) of COA Circular No. 2014-002; and
Ensure compliance with the laws on procurement embodied in RA 9184 and keep themselves abreast with the latest issuances of the GPPB especially on emergency procurement so as to not repeat the deficiencies noted.

We recommended that the Management submit documents and justifications to support the propriety of the hazard pay claims and revisit the guidelines and conditions on the grant the COVID-19 Hazard Pay issued by the Department of Budget and Management to avoid future infractions and to recompute the COVID-19 Hazard Pay and refund the overpayment.

We recommended that the Municipal Government establish a specific set of guidelines aligned with the provisions of DSWD MC No. 2014-002 in the grant of financial assistance. To regulate the distribution of financial assistance, we also recommend that beneficiaries should be properly screened and selected using proper eligibility criteria to ensure that the intended individuals are prioritized. Moreover, we recommended the Municipality to adhere to the NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1 with regards to the allocation and utilization of the LDRRMF.

We recommended that the Management direct the Property and Accounting Units to investigate, correct, adjust and record the unreconciled PPE balances between the books and inventory report and undertake the procedural guidelines enumerated in COA Circular No. 2020-006 for the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances to come up with more reliable account balances to be presented in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Management direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer together with the Municipal Assessor, the immediate analysis, reconciliation and updating of their records and reports to determine the cause of discrepancy amounting to ₱135,656,063.04 to reflect reliable and accurate RPT and SET Receivables of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Management:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the Bank Reconciliation Statements and submit the same with supporting documents within the prescribed period;
Base the balances of the Cash in Bank – Local Currency – Current Account and Time Deposits accounts from the resulting adjusted balances of the Bank Reconciliation Statements; and
Instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile their respective records at least every quarter.

We recommended that the Management:
Revisit their organizational structure and staffing pattern to update and reflect their current manpower needs;
Analyze the remaining allowable allotment for Personnel Services and consider the creation of relevant additional plantilla positions that will add value to their Municipality; and
Fill-up the vacant plantilla positions by hiring new employees or expedite promotion of deserving employees to enhance its services to attain full implementation of programs, plans and activities of the local government unit for the optimal benefit of the constituents as well as to provide equal job opportunities to the community.

We recommended that the Management:
Require the Municipal Assessor to expedite the titling of recorded parcels of land to establish ownership thereof and to fully protect the interest of the Municipality; and
Provide the Office of the Audit Team Leader with Quarterly Status Report with supporting documents on the status of the application for land registration

We recommended that the Management require the Municipal Accountant to maintain a special account for 20% DF through the use of complete subsidiary ledgers and prepare the corresponding financial statements which shall form part of the schedules of the General Fund.

We recommended that the Management direct the BAC Chairman to:
Prepare, consolidate and submit the required Procurement Monitoring Report within the prescribed submission in the GPPB Circular not only to the Department of the Interior and Local Government for purposes of compliance report for the Seal of Good Local Governance but on the obligations of the agency on the full disclosure and sustainability of transparent practice on how the public funds are managed, disbursed and used; and
Monitor each procurement activities specified in the Annual Procurement Plan (APP) and reflect the information in the Procurement Monitoring Report (PMR) as well as the timeline on all procurement activities.

We recommended that the Management direct the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board to provide for schemes that would improve solid waste management in the municipality and strictly comply with the following:
Strictly enforce waste segregation at source in order to achieve the primary objective to protect the environment and public health from potential hazard of solid wastes;
Promote Solid Waste Management Awareness through proactive Information and Education Campaigns (IEC) in coordination with the Barangay Solid Waste Management Committees and academic institutions; and
Monitor the constructed MRFs in different areas and barangays within the Municipality to see if these facilities are operating effectively and serve its intended purpose.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Instruct the Accounting and Property Office to review the existing records
of the PPE accounts, maintain perpetual inventory records and conduct
periodic reconciliation of balances in accordance with the NGAS Manual
for LGUs;
b. Direct the General Services Officer to locate the unserviceable properties
and to present the said unserviceable properties in the IIRUP; and
c. Cause the immediate and proper disposal of all unserviceable properties
pursuant to Section 79 of PD 1445 and the Manual on Disposal of
Government Property.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to prepare and submit Bank Reconciliation Statements within the
prescribed period. We have likewise recommended for the Municipal Accountant
and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile their respective records at least every quarter.

We have recommended that Management:

a. Strictly adhere with accounting policies and standards set by the
Commission such that all lawful expenditures and obligations incurred
during the year shall be taken-up in the accounts of that year;
b. Depart from overusing the Prior Period Adjustment Account by exercising
the vigilance and diligence which are required of them in accounting for
the accounts of the Municipality; and
c. Provide justification on why the Prior Period Adjustment Account was
debited to that extent.

We have reiterated our previous recommendation that in order to enforce
collection, Management send demand letters to the last known addresses of the
borrowers and thereafter exhaust all remedies to collect the receivables. We
recommended further that for fair presentation of receivables, Management
assess the actual status of the dormant receivables; confirm with existing
debtors and request for write-off in accordance with Commission on Audit
Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016, if applicable.

Management was also enjoined to adhere with the general and specific
guidelines cited in COA Circular 2016-005 in order to minimize the incidence
of receivables falling past due.

We have recommended that the Management:

a) Ensure proper granting, utilization and liquidation of fund transfers to
NGOs/POs;
b) Institute action against NGO/PO’s and their officers/organizers that failed
to implement the programs and projects funded by the Municipality;
c) Provide Allowance for Impairment for the dormant receivables from the
NGO/PO pursuant to the provisions of PPSAS 29; and
d) Submit a request for write-off duly supported with the documentary
requirements for writing-off dormant accounts.

We have recommended that the Management:

a) Direct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile their claim to the Provincial
Government of La Union and exert all efforts to establish the existence
such claim;
b) Adhere with the general and specific guidelines cited in COA Circulars 88-
291 and 2016-005 in order to minimize the incidence of receivables falling
past due; and
c) Assess the actual status of the dormant receivables, confirm with existing
debtors and request for write-off in accordance with Commission on Audit
Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016, if applicable.

We have recommended that Management comply and submit the
documentary requirements to support the payment of the purchased lot
pursuant to COA Circular 2012-001 and PD 1445. The Accounting Office shall
likewise record the purchased lot at P2,000,000.00 to reflect the actual cost
incurred during acquisition.

We further recommended that the Accounting Office prepare and maintain PPLC for each class and RPLC for Land account inclusive of significant details to support the financial information in the GL and the financial statements.

We recommended that expenditures for other purposes be charged to the General Fund. We recommended further that in line with the existing guidelines on the  utilization of the SEF, Management and the LSB refrain from accommodating charges which are not related to the specified allowable expenses.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer:
Always require posting of warranty security in any acceptable form for completed infrastructure projects in accordance with Section 62 of  the 2016 IRR of RA 9184;
Henceforth establish a system and maintain records on specific warranty periods and security for each procurement of civil works.
We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Inspectorate Team to conduct inspection of completed projects after the one (1) year defects liability period and issue the corresponding Certificate of Final Acceptance to the contractors if no defect /deficiency  was detected.

We recommended that in accordance with LBC No. 111, Management consider the full adoption of the guide in operating its existing LEEs in view of their critical roles in the LGU development and their significant impact to good local governance.

We recommended that  in order to maximize the use of available resources to boost farmers’ income and productivity,  Management enhance coordination among operating divisions; monitor the utilization of funds received by the Municipal Government  pursuant to RA 8240;  and initiate programs and projects in accordance with the purpose of the funds.

Considering the substantial investment involved in the provision of  potable water system projects and in cognizance of  the equally significant impact of the projects in addressing the plight of waterless communities, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Implementing Unit to enhance monitoring of  ongoing projects to determine if advisories need to be issued  in accordance with DILG MC 2015-74 to ensure timely completion of the projects.

We, therefore, recommended and  Management agreed to ensure availability of resources and inclusion of the lacking items in the formulation of the LDRRMFIP in  compliance with the minimum preparedness standards set forth under the NDPP not only to support the initiatives of the DILG but most significantly to avert the loss of lives during disasters.

We recommended that Management closely monitor the ongoing civil works  for the conversion of controlled dump site to sanitary landfill,  not only to ensure compliance with the IRR of ESWM Act and accomplish the commitment under the Ten-year ESWMP but also to have assurance that the rehabilitation is in accordance to designs geared for maximum control over environmental impacts.

For the proper management and handling of the PPE, we recommended that Management:
•	Complete the required annual physical count by type of the PPE as of December of every year.  The actual count may be undertaken any month during the current year, but has to be updated at year-end.  This procedure is a must in order to know the presence/physical existence of fixed assets and their conditions; and to identify, segregate and reclassify unserviceable assets.  Reconciliation of the result of the count with the accounting records is to be undertaken to effect adjustments; and to pin point  responsibility/accountability/liability for missing PPEs.

•	For control and monitoring, the GSO  make use of ICS for semi-expendable items to avoid loss, as these are usually small items.
